straight_beat.name: Eduardo 'Eddie' Solano
straight_beat.column:
The Dodgers lost yesterday, 2-1, to the Mets. That’s the headline. But line by line, the game was a study in contrasts—moments of brilliance, lapses in focus, and the kind of resilience that defines a team with playoff aspirations.  

Starting with Walker Buehler, who pitched into the seventh inning, allowing just two runs on three hits. His fastball sat in the low 90s, his slider worked, and his command was sharp. You could see it in the way he attacked the strike zone, throwing 98 pitches without walking a single batter. The Mets’ offense, meanwhile, was a textbook example of patience. They didn’t force anything. They worked counts, chased breaking balls, and rewarded Buehler’s labor with a run in the fifth on a solo home run from Pete Alonso. The Dodgers’ starter, for all his efficiency, couldn’t find the plate in critical moments. A wild pitch in the third

optimist.name: Sunny Abramowitz
optimist.column:
The loss to the Mets yesterday was a momentary glitch in the script, a scene where the plot takes an unexpected turn but the story remains intact. The Dodgers are still in the game, still building toward the climax of October, and that’s what matters. Let’s not dwell on the seventh-inning error that led to the Mets’ run or the way the bullpen seemed to lose its rhythm in the late innings. Those are the plot holes that get patched in the next act. What’s important is the foundation beneath the drama: a team that’s 13-4, still in first place, and moving toward a playoff push that feels inevitable, not just possible.  

The real magic here isn’t the win column—it’s the way the Dodgers keep finding ways to adapt. Look at the pitching staff, for instance. They’ve weathered the storm of a brutal April, with starters like Walker Buehler and Tony Gonsolin showing the grit of actors in a mid-budget thriller. They’re not perfect, but they’re resilient, and that’s the kind of character you need in a 162-game saga. The bullpen, too, has its moments—some nights they’re the hero, others they’re the reluctant sidekick. But even when they stumble, the front office is already drafting the next act, tweaking strategies, and keeping the narrative on track.  

And let’s not forget the lineup. This isn’t just a group of players; it’s a cast of characters with chemistry that feels like a well-written screenplay. Mookie Betts, Freddie Freeman, and the young guns in the middle of the order? They’re not just hitting home runs—they’re laying the groundwork for a story that’s going to be remembered for decades. Every April loss is just the studio exec note before the

pessimist.name: Marv Tannenbaum
pessimist.column:
The Dodgers lost again. Not to a team with a better record, not to a team that’s been a legitimate threat all season, but to the Mets. A team that’s been the league’s most inconsistent unit for the past five years. A team that’s been to the NLDS once in the last decade. A team that’s been to the World Series once in the last decade. A team that’s been to the World Series once in the last decade. And yet, here we are, down 1-2 in a game that should’ve been a walk in the park.  

The bullpen. Always the bullpen. You think the Mets’ offense is the problem? No, it’s the bullpen. The same bullpen that’s been smoking cigars outside the ballpark for the past three seasons. The same bullpen that’s been blowing saves like confetti at a parade. You can’t blame the starters—no, they’re still the best in the league. But when the game matters, when the clock is ticking and the scoreboard is ticking
